file_name: Non Disclosure Agreement - CP Specific NDA (CreativeNDA-CPS121.1014).txt

perturbation.original_text: 5.2 Each Recipi ent shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified the Disclosing Party and its Group at all times against all liabilities, costs (includ ing legal costs on an indemnity basis), expenses, damages and losses (including any direct, indirect or consequential losses, loss of profit, loss of reputation and all interest, penalties and other reasonable costs and expenses suffered or incurred by the Disclosing Party and/or its Group) a rising from any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 Each Recipi ent shall indemnify the Disclosing Party against direct damages arising from a material breach of this Agreement by the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: This change severely limits the scope of indemnification. Removing 'keep fully indemnified', 'at all times', and narrowing the damages to 'direct damages arising from a material breach' significantly reduces the protection afforded to the Disclosing Party. By removing the phrase including legal costs on an indemnity basis, legal costs are no longer covered.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware contract law regarding indemnification, specifically the principle of full indemnification to make the indemnitee whole.
perturbation.law_citation: Judlo, Inc. v. PMG Cap. Corp., 2016 WL 4154963, at *12 (Del. Ch. Aug. 5, 2016)
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware courts generally interpret indemnification clauses to provide full indemnity, covering all losses and expenses. By limiting the indemnification to 'direct damages arising from a material breach', the clause contradicts this principle and reduces the Disclosing Party's protection.

General Provisions Part 1 General Provisions Â§ 1-101. Short titles. (a) This subtitle may be cited as the Uniform Commercial Code. (b) This article may be cited as Uniform Commercial Code â General Provisions. 5A Del. C. 1953, Â§Â§ â1-101;Â 55 Del. Laws, c. 349 ;Â 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-102. Scope of article. This article applies to a transaction to the extent that it is governed by another article of the Uniform Commercial Code. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-103. Construction of Uniform Commercial Code to promote its purposes and policies; applicability of supplemental principles of law. (a) The Uniform Commercial Code must be liberally construed and applied to promote its underlying purposes and policies, which are: (1) To simplify, clarify, and modernize the law governing commercial transactions; (2) To permit the continued expansion of commercial practices through custom, usage, and agreement of the parties; and (3) To make uniform the law among the various jurisdictions. (b) Unless displaced by the particular provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code, the principles of law and equity, including the law merchant and the law relative to capacity to contract, principal and agent, estoppel, fraud, misrepresentation, duress, coercion, mistake, bankruptcy, and other validating or invalidating cause supplement its provisions. 5A Del. C. 1953, Â§Â§ â1-102;Â 55 Del. Laws, c. 349 ;Â 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-104. Construction against implied repeal. The Uniform Commercial Code being a general act intended as a unified coverage of its subject matter, no part of it shall be deemed to be impliedly repealed by subsequent legislation if such construction can reasonably be avoided. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-105. Severability. If any provision or clause of the Uniform Commercial Code or its application to any person or circumstance is held invalid, the invalidity does not affect other provisions or applications of the Uniform Commercial Code which can be given effect without the invalid provision or application, and to this end the provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code are severable. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-106. Use of singular and plural; gender. In the Uniform Commercial Code, unless the statutory context otherwise requires: (1) Words in the singular number include the plural, and those in the plural include the singular; and (2) Words of any gender also refer to any other gender. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-107. Section captions. Section captions are part of the Uniform Commercial Code. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-108. perturbation.justification: The original text provides broad indemnification for the Disclosing Party, covering all liabilities, costs (including legal costs), expenses, damages, and losses arising from any breach by the Recipient. The changed text significantly narrows this scope, limiting indemnification to 'direct damages arising from a material breach'. This omission of key protections, such as legal costs and indirect losses, creates a legal contradiction. While the provided snippet from Delaware Code Title 6 does not directly address indemnification clauses, the Law Explanation states that Delaware courts generally interpret these clauses to provide full indemnity, encompassing all losses and expenses. By restricting the indemnification, the modified clause contradicts this principle and reduces the Disclosing Party's protection, thus creating a legal contradiction by omission. This falls under Omission Legal Contradiction, where essential information is removed creating legal loopholes and reducing legally mandated protection.

perturbation.original_text: 2.3 A Party may disclose Confidential Information to the extent such Confidential Information is required to be disclosed by law, by any governmental or other regulatory authority (including, without limitation any relevant securities exchange) or by a Court or other authority of competent jurisdiction provided that, to the extent it is legally permitted to do so, it gives the other Party as much notice of this disclosure as possible and, where notice of disclosure is not prohibited and is given in accordance with this Clause 2.3 , it takes into account the reasonable requests of the other Party in relation to the content of this disclosure, to the extent that it is legally permitted to do so.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.3 A Party may disclose Confidential Information to the extent such Confidential Information is required to be disclosed by law, by any governmental or other regulatory authority (including, without limitation any relevant securities exchange) or by a Court or other authority of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'provided that, to the extent it is legally permitted to do so, it gives the other Party as much notice of this disclosure as possible and, where notice of disclosure is not prohibited and is given in accordance with this Clause 2.3 , it takes into account the reasonable requests of the other Party in relation to the content of this disclosure, to the extent that it is legally permitted to do so', the clause removes the obligation to provide notice and consider reasonable requests before disclosing confidential information when required by law. Delaware law implies a duty of good faith and fair dealing in contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_citation: Dunlap v. State Farm Fire & Cas. Co., 878 A.2d 434, 442 (Del. 2005)
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Removing the notice provision could be construed as a violation of this covenant, particularly if the disclosure could be managed in a way that minimizes harm to the Disclosing Party while still complying with legal requirements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 18 > Chapter 11 Authenticated PDF Â§ 1127 Â§ 1128 Â§ 1129 Â§ 1130 TITLE 18 Insurance Code Insurance CHAPTER 11. Assets and Liabilities Subchapter IV. Valuation of Assets Â§ 1127. Valuation of bonds. (a) All bonds or other evidences of debt having a fixed term and rate of interest held by an insurer may, if amply secured and not in default as to principal or interest, be valued as follows: (1) If purchased at par, at the par value; (2) If purchased above or below par, on the basis of the purchase price adjusted so as to bring the value to par at maturity and so as to yield in the meantime the effective rate of interest at which the purchase was made, or in lieu of such method, according to such accepted method of valuation as is approved by the Commissioner; (3) Purchase price shall in no case be taken at a higher figure than the actual market value at the time of purchase, plus actual brokerage, transfer, postage or express charges paid in the acquisition of such securities; (4) Unless otherwise provided by valuation established or approved by the Commissioner, no such security shall be carried at above the call price for the entire issue during any period within which the security may be so called. (b) Notwithstanding any other provision of this section, no bond or other evidence of debt shall be valued in excess of the value established by the National Association of Insurance Commissionersâ Capital Markets and Investment Analysis Office. 18 Del. C. 1953, Â§ â1114;Â 56 Del. Laws, c. 380, Â§ â1 ;Â 69 Del. Laws, c. 92, Â§ â4 ;Â 80 Del. Laws, c. 117, Â§ â2 ;Â 83 Del. Laws, c. 184, Â§ 3 ;Â Â§ 1128. Valuation of other securities. (a) Securities, other than those referred to in Â§â1127 of this title, held by an insurer shall be valued, in the discretion of the Commissioner, at their market value, or at their appraised value, or at prices determined by the Commissioner as representing their fair market value. (b) Preferred or guaranteed stocks or shares while paying full dividends may be carried at a fixed value in lieu of market value, at the discretion of the Commissioner and in accordance with such method of computation as he or she may approve. perturbation.justification: The original text includes a provision for notice and consideration of requests before disclosing confidential information when required by law. The changed text removes this provision. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that removing this notice provision could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address good faith, the Perturbation and Law explanations clearly indicate that the omission could be construed as a violation of Delaware law regarding contractual obligations. The removal creates a direct conflict with the implied duty of good faith.
